publications.title: TCRs used in cancer gene therapy cross-react with MART-1/Melan-A tumor antigens via distinct mechanisms.
publications.authors: Borbulevych OY, Santhanagopolan SM, Hossain M, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: This study determined crystal structures of two engineered TCRs (DMF4 and DMF5) in complex with two distinct peptide epitopes from the melanoma antigen MART-1/Melan-A, both presented by HLA-A*0201. The structures revealed that these clinically-used cancer immunotherapy TCRs achieve cross-reactivity through divergent mechanisms: DMF4 accommodates the two peptides by altering its binding orientation, whereas DMF5 binds both peptides identically. DMF5's invariant binding mode correlates with higher affinity for both ligands and superior functional avidity, demonstrating that simple, orientation-independent recognition is mechanistically advantageous. These findings elucidate how engineered TCRs recognize multiple tumor epitopes and establish structural principles for rational design of TCRs with improved avidity in adoptive cell therapy for melanoma and other cancers.

publications.title: Computational design of the affinity and specificity of a therapeutic T cell receptor.
publications.authors: Pierce BG, Hellman LM, Hossain M, Singh NK, Vander Kooi CW, Weng Z, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: This computational design study engineered the clinically relevant TCR DMF5, which recognizes Melan-A/MART-1 melanoma epitopes presented by HLA-A2, to achieve substantially improved binding affinity. The researchers used flexible and rigid molecular modeling to guide mutations and identified a double mutant that exhibits a 400-fold affinity enhancement for the cognate pMHC ligand while maintaining specificity—binding no detectable off-target ligands. The high-resolution structure of the optimized variant revealed minimal conformational perturbation, validating the fidelity of the computational approach. This work demonstrates that rational design can simultaneously enhance TCR affinity for cancer antigens and preserve peptide specificity, addressing a key constraint in TCR-based cancer immunotherapy where naive TCRs have only modest binding strength and affinity-maturation efforts risk unwanted cross-reactivity.

publications.title: Improving T Cell Receptor On-Target Specificity via Structure-Guided Design.
publications.journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy
publications.authors: Hellman LM, Foley KC, Singh NK, Alonso JA, Riley TP, Devlin JR, Ayres CM, Keller GLJ, Zhang Y, Vander Kooi CW, Nishimura MI, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined the MART-1-specific TCR DMF5 bound to cognate peptide–MHC-I complexes to address a critical challenge in TCR-based immunotherapy: cross-reactivity toward off-target peptides. The authors demonstrated that affinity-enhancing mutations paradoxically increase cross-recognition of structurally similar peptides, and developed a structure-guided design approach to improve specificity independent of binding affinity. Applied to DMF5, this method reduced cross-reactivity against MART-1 homologs and eliminated recognition of divergent, unrelated epitopes. This work matters for TCR engineering because off-target cross-reactivity can cause immune toxicity in therapeutic applications; the structure-guided strategy offers a rational path to develop more specific TCR therapeutics without the unintended consequences of traditional affinity maturation.
